instruction: Build a Rust HTTP API client using reqwest with retry logic, tower middleware, and authentication.
output:
<think>
Rust API clients use reqwest for HTTP and tower for middleware. reqwest supports connection pooling, TLS, cookies, and async. Tower provides retry, rate limiting, and timeout middleware as composable layers.

The pattern: build a reqwest::Client with connection pool settings, wrap in tower service layers for cross-cutting concerns. Use typed response deserialization with serde.
</think>

```rust
use reqwest::{Client, Response, StatusCode};
use serde::de::DeserializeOwned;
use serde::Deserialize;
use std::time::Duration;
use tokio::time::sleep;

#[derive(Clone)]
pub struct ApiClient {
    client: Client,
    base_url: String,
    api_key: String,
    max_retries: u32,
}

#[derive(Debug, thiserror::Error)]
pub enum ApiError {
    #[error("HTTP error: {0}")]
    Http(#[from] reqwest::Error),
    #[error("API error {status}: {body}")]
    Api { status: u16, body: String },
    #[error("Rate limited, retry after {retry_after}s")]
    RateLimit { retry_after: u64 },
}

impl ApiClient {
    pub fn new(base_url: &str, api_key: &str) -> Self {
        let client = Client::builder()
            .timeout(Duration::from_secs(30))
            .pool_max_idle_per_host(10)
            .pool_idle_timeout(Duration::from_secs(90))
            .build()
            .expect("Failed to build HTTP client");
        Self {
            client,
            base_url: base_url.to_string(),
            api_key: api_key.to_string(),
            max_retries: 3,
        }
    }

    async fn request<T: DeserializeOwned>(
        &self, method: reqwest::Method, path: &str,
    ) -> Result<T, ApiError> {
        let url = format!("{}{}", self.base_url, path);
        let mut last_err = None;
        for attempt in 0..=self.max_retries {
            let resp = self.client
                .request(method.clone(), &url)
                .bearer_auth(&self.api_key)
                .send()
                .await?;
            match resp.status() {
                s if s == StatusCode::TOO_MANY_REQUESTS => {
                    let wait = resp.headers()
                        .get("retry-after")
                        .and_then(|v| v.to_str().ok())
                        .and_then(|v| v.parse::<u64>().ok())
                        .unwrap_or(2u64.pow(attempt));
                    sleep(Duration::from_secs(wait)).await;
                    continue;
                }
                s if s.is_server_error() && attempt < self.max_retries => {
                    sleep(Duration::from_secs(2u64.pow(attempt))).await;
                    continue;
                }
                s if s.is_success() => return Ok(resp.json::<T>().await?),
                s => {
                    let body = resp.text().await.unwrap_or_default();
                    last_err = Some(ApiError::Api { status: s.as_u16(), body });
                }
            }
        }
        Err(last_err.unwrap_or(ApiError::Api { status: 0, body: "max retries".into() }))
    }

    pub async fn get<T: DeserializeOwned>(&self, path: &str) -> Result<T, ApiError> {
        self.request(reqwest::Method::GET, path).await
    }
}

#[derive(Deserialize)]
struct User { id: u64, name: String, email: String }

#[tokio::main]
async fn main() -> Result<(), ApiError> {
    let client = ApiClient::new("https://api.example.com", "sk-123");
    let user: User = client.get("/users/1").await?;
    println!("User: {} ({})", user.name, user.email);
    Ok(())
}
```

instruction: Show Rust patterns for streaming API responses and handling large payloads efficiently.
output:
<think>
Streaming responses in Rust avoids loading entire responses into memory. reqwest provides bytes_stream() for streaming downloads. For uploads, use reqwest::Body::wrap_stream.

Key patterns: stream response body chunk by chunk, pipe to file or processor, handle backpressure with bounded channels.
</think>

```rust
use futures_util::StreamExt;
use reqwest::Client;
use std::path::Path;
use tokio::fs::File;
use tokio::io::AsyncWriteExt;

async fn download_file(client: &Client, url: &str, dest: &Path) -> Result<u64, Box<dyn std::error::Error>> {
    let resp = client.get(url).send().await?.error_for_status()?;
    let total = resp.content_length().unwrap_or(0);
    let mut file = File::create(dest).await?;
    let mut stream = resp.bytes_stream();
    let mut downloaded: u64 = 0;
    while let Some(chunk) = stream.next().await {
        let bytes = chunk?;
        file.write_all(&bytes).await?;
        downloaded += bytes.len() as u64;
        if total > 0 {
            let pct = (downloaded as f64 / total as f64) * 100.0;
            eprint!("\rDownloading: {pct:.1}%");
        }
    }
    file.flush().await?;
    eprintln!("\nDone: {downloaded} bytes");
    Ok(downloaded)
}

async fn stream_json_lines(client: &Client, url: &str) -> Result<Vec<serde_json::Value>, Box<dyn std::error::Error>> {
    let resp = client.get(url).send().await?.error_for_status()?;
    let body = resp.text().await?;
    let items: Vec<serde_json::Value> = body
        .lines()
        .filter(|l| !l.is_empty())
        .filter_map(|l| serde_json::from_str(l).ok())
        .collect();
    Ok(items)
}

#[tokio::main]
async fn main() -> Result<(), Box<dyn std::error::Error>> {
    let client = Client::new();
    download_file(&client, "https://example.com/large.bin", Path::new("/tmp/large.bin")).await?;
    Ok(())
}
```

instruction: Show how to build a Rust API client with typed request/response models using serde and generics.
output:
<think>
Type-safe API clients in Rust use serde for automatic JSON serialization/deserialization. Generic methods accept a type parameter that implements Deserialize, ensuring compile-time type safety for API responses.

The pattern: define response structs with #[derive(Deserialize)], use generic request methods, handle errors with a custom error enum.
</think>

```rust
use reqwest::Client;
use serde::{Deserialize, Serialize};
use std::marker::PhantomData;

#[derive(Debug, Deserialize)]
pub struct User {
    pub id: u64,
    pub name: String,
    pub email: String,
}

#[derive(Debug, Deserialize)]
pub struct Paginated<T> {
    pub items: Vec<T>,
    pub total: u64,
    pub page: u32,
    pub has_next: bool,
}

#[derive(Debug, Serialize)]
pub struct CreateUserRequest {
    pub name: String,
    pub email: String,
}

pub struct TypedClient {
    client: Client,
    base_url: String,
    token: String,
}

impl TypedClient {
    pub fn new(base_url: &str, token: &str) -> Self {
        Self {
            client: Client::new(),
            base_url: base_url.to_string(),
            token: token.to_string(),
        }
    }

    async fn get<T: serde::de::DeserializeOwned>(&self, path: &str) -> Result<T, reqwest::Error> {
        self.client
            .get(format!("{}{}", self.base_url, path))
            .bearer_auth(&self.token)
            .send()
            .await?
            .error_for_status()?
            .json::<T>()
            .await
    }

    async fn post<B: Serialize, T: serde::de::DeserializeOwned>(
        &self, path: &str, body: &B,
    ) -> Result<T, reqwest::Error> {
        self.client
            .post(format!("{}{}", self.base_url, path))
            .bearer_auth(&self.token)
            .json(body)
            .send()
            .await?
            .error_for_status()?
            .json::<T>()
            .await
    }

    pub async fn get_user(&self, id: u64) -> Result<User, reqwest::Error> {
        self.get(&format!("/users/{id}")).await
    }

    pub async fn list_users(&self, page: u32) -> Result<Paginated<User>, reqwest::Error> {
        self.get(&format!("/users?page={page}")).await
    }

    pub async fn create_user(&self, req: &CreateUserRequest) -> Result<User, reqwest::Error> {
        self.post("/users", req).await
    }
}
```

instruction: Show Rust patterns for API client middleware using tower layers for retry, timeout, and logging.
output:
<think>
Tower is Rust's middleware framework. Each Layer wraps a Service, adding behavior. For API clients: RetryLayer for automatic retries, TimeoutLayer for request timeouts, and a custom logging layer.

The beauty of tower is composability -- layers stack like middleware chains. Each layer only knows about the layer below it.
</think>

```rust
use std::time::Duration;
use tower::{ServiceBuilder, ServiceExt};
use tower::retry::{Retry, RetryLayer};
use tower::timeout::TimeoutLayer;
use tower::limit::RateLimitLayer;

// Custom retry policy
#[derive(Clone)]
struct RetryPolicy {
    max_retries: usize,
}

impl<Req: Clone, Res, E> tower::retry::Policy<Req, Res, E> for RetryPolicy {
    type Future = futures_util::future::Ready<()>;

    fn retry(&mut self, _req: &mut Req, result: &mut Result<Res, E>) -> Option<Self::Future> {
        if self.max_retries == 0 {
            return None;
        }
        if result.is_err() {
            self.max_retries -= 1;
            Some(futures_util::future::ready(()))
        } else {
            None
        }
    }

    fn clone_request(&mut self, req: &Req) -> Option<Req> {
        Some(req.clone())
    }
}

// Build layered service
// let service = ServiceBuilder::new()
//     .layer(TimeoutLayer::new(Duration::from_secs(30)))
//     .layer(RateLimitLayer::new(10, Duration::from_secs(1)))
//     .layer(RetryLayer::new(RetryPolicy { max_retries: 3 }))
//     .service(hyper_client);
//
// The request flows: Timeout -> RateLimit -> Retry -> HTTP Client
// Each layer adds its behavior transparently.

// Logging middleware example
use std::task::{Context, Poll};
use std::pin::Pin;
use std::future::Future;

#[derive(Clone)]
struct LoggingService<S> {
    inner: S,
    prefix: String,
}

impl<S, Req> tower::Service<Req> for LoggingService<S>
where
    S: tower::Service<Req>,
    Req: std::fmt::Debug,
{
    type Response = S::Response;
    type Error = S::Error;
    type Future = S::Future;

    fn poll_ready(&mut self, cx: &mut Context<'_>) -> Poll<Result<(), Self::Error>> {
        self.inner.poll_ready(cx)
    }

    fn call(&mut self, req: Req) -> Self::Future {
        eprintln!("[{}] Request: {:?}", self.prefix, req);
        self.inner.call(req)
    }
}

fn main() {
    println!("Tower middleware layers compose like function composition");
}
```
